request.question: What is the title of the work, based on Philippa Gregory's historical novel series "The Cousins' War" did Max Irons take part?

title: The Kingmaker's Daughter
The White Queen (novel)
The King's Curse
The Women of the Cousins' War
The White Princess
The Queen's Fool
The White Queen (TV series)
The Red Queen (novel)
Max Irons
The Lady of the Rivers
sentences: The Kingmaker's Daughter is a 2012 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
It is the story of Anne Neville, wife of Richard III of England.

The 2013 BBC One television series "The White Queen" is a 10-part adaptation of Gregory's novels "The White Queen" (2009), "The Red Queen" (2010) and "The Kingmaker's Daughter", and features Faye Marsay as Anne Neville.
The White Queen is a 2009 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, the first of her series "The Cousins' War".
It tells the story of Elizabeth Woodville, queen consort of King Edward IV of England.

The 2013 BBC One television series "The White Queen" is a 10-part adaptation of Gregory's novels "The White Queen", "The Red Queen" (2010) and "The Kingmaker's Daughter" (2012), and features Rebecca Ferguson as Elizabeth Woodville.
The King's Curse is a 2014 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
A direct sequel to "The White Princess", it follows the adult life of Margaret Pole, Countess of Salisbury, the daughter of George Plantagenet, 1st Duke of Clarence and Isabel Neville.
The Women of the Cousins' War: The Duchess, the Queen and the King's Mother is a 2011 book by historical novelist Philippa Gregory and historians David Baldwin and Michael Jones.
It is a non-fiction work that explores the lives of three prominent women of the historical Wars of the Roses, all of whom Gregory has featured in her "Cousins' War" series of novels.
The White Princess is a 2013 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
It is the story of Elizabeth of York, daughter of Edward IV of England and Elizabeth Woodville, and later wife of Henry VII and mother of Henry VIII.
The Queen's Fool by Philippa Gregory is a 2004 historical fiction novel.
Set between 1548 and 1558, it is part of Philippa Gregory's Tudor series.
The series includes "The Boleyn Inheritance".

The novel chronicles the changing fortunes of Mary I of England and her half-sister Elizabeth through the eyes of the fictional Hannah Green, a Marrano girl escaping to England from Spain where her mother was burned at the stake for being Jewish.
Hannah is discovered by Robert Dudley and John Dee and subsequently begged as a fool to Edward VI.
She witnesses and becomes caught up the intrigues of the young king's court, and later those of his sisters.
As Mary, Elizabeth, and Robert Dudley use Hannah to gather information on their rivals and further their own aims, the novel can plausibly present each side in the complex story.
"The Queen's Fool" follows Hannah from ages fourteen to nineteen, and her coming-of-age is interspersed among the historical narrative (see Bildungsroman).
The book reached # 29 on the "New York Times" Best Seller list and had sold 165,000 copies within three weeks of its release.

The White Queen is a British television drama series in ten parts, based on Philippa Gregory's historical novel series "The Cousins' War" ("The White Queen", "The Red Queen", and "The Kingmaker's Daughter").
The first episode was premiered on BBC One on 16 June 2013 in the United Kingdom.
It was first broadcast in the United States on Starz on 9 August 2013.
The Red Queen is a 2010 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, the second of her series "The Cousins' War".
It is the story of Margaret Beaufort, mother of Henry VII of England.

The 2013 BBC One television series "The White Queen" is a 10-part adaptation of Gregory's novels "The White Queen" (2009), "The Red Queen" and "The Kingmaker's Daughter" (2012), and features Amanda Hale as Margaret Beaufort.
Maximilian Paul Diarmuid "Max" Irons (born 17 October 1985) is an English-Irish actor and model.
He is known for his roles in "Red Riding Hood" (2011), "The White Queen" (2013), "The Host" (2013), and "The Riot Club" (2014).
The Lady of the Rivers is a 2011 historical novel by Philippa Gregory, part of her series "The Cousins' War".
The story is narrated by Jacquetta of Luxembourg, mother of Elizabeth Woodville, and covers the reign of the Lancastrian King Henry VI.
The novel serves as a prequel to Gregory's "The White Queen" (2009), the story of Elizabeth's reign as Queen of England.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - Philippa Gregory's series and adapted work: passage [3] states "'The White Queen' ... based on Philippa Gregory's historical novel series 'The Cousins' War'." (passage [3]). Passage [6] shows "'The White Queen' is a 2009 historical novel" (passage [6]). Passages [2] and [4] list the related novels "'The Kingmaker's Daughter' (2012)" and "'The Red Queen' (2010)" respectively (passages [2], [4]).
  - Max Irons: no passage mentions "Max Irons" or any cast list.
- Ambiguities:
  - Candidate works in the passages include the TV adaptation "The White Queen (TV series)" and the novels "The White Queen", "The Red Queen", and "The Kingmaker's Daughter" as titled items. No passage provides casting, and the expected "Max Irons" page/filmography is missing from the supplied set.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as given in the passages): "The White Queen (TV series)". 
  - Missing fact needed: whether Max Irons appears in that adaptation (or in any titled adaptation listed) — specifically the cast entry showing "Max Irons" and the exact work title he took part in.
- Retrieval direction (prioritized next hops):
  1) Open the "Max Irons" page (lede/infobox and 'Filmography' or 'Television' sections) — search keywords: "Max Irons filmography" / "Max Irons The White Queen".
  2) Open "The White Queen (TV series)" page — check the 'Cast' section and lede/infobox for actor listings and episode credits.
  3) If not found, fetch "Max Irons" filmography subpages or alternative adaptations' cast lists (e.g., "The White Princess (TV series)" or specific episode cast pages) — search keywords: "Max Irons cast list BBC 2013" or "Max Irons television roles".
Final answer is deferred until those pages (especially Max Irons' page and the TV series cast section) are retrieved and checked for his participation.
response.hop2_query: Max Irons The White Queen cast
hop2_docs: Max Irons | Maximilian Paul Diarmuid "Max" Irons (born 17 October 1985) is an English-Irish actor and model.  He is known for his roles in "Red Riding Hood" (2011), "The White Queen" (2013), "The Host" (2013), and "The Riot Club" (2014).

Cast iron | Cast iron is a group of iron-carbon alloys with a carbon content greater than 2%.  Its usefulness derives from its relatively low melting temperature.  The alloy constituents affect its colour when fractured: white cast iron has carbide impurities which allow cracks to pass straight through, grey cast iron has graphite flakes which deflect a passing crack and initiate countless new cracks as the material breaks, and ductile cast iron has spherical graphite "nodules" which stop the crack from further progressing.

Malleable iron | Malleable iron is cast as white iron, the structure being a metastable carbide in a pearlitic matrix.  Through an annealing heat treatment, the brittle structure as first cast is transformed into the malleable form.  Carbon agglomerates into small roughly spherical aggregates of graphite leaving a matrix of ferrite or pearlite according to the exact heat treatment used.  Three basic types of malleable iron are recognized within the casting industry: "blackheart" malleable iron, "whiteheart" malleable iron and "pearlitic" malleable iron.

Ductile iron | Ductile iron, also known as ductile cast iron, nodular cast iron, spheroidal graphite iron, spheroidal graphite cast iron and SG iron, is a type of graphite-rich cast iron discovered in 1943 by Keith Millis.  While most varieties of cast iron are brittle, ductile iron has much more impact and fatigue resistance, due to its "nodular graphite" inclusions.

Pot metal | Pot metal—also known as "monkey metal", "white metal", or "die-cast zinc"—is a colloquial term that refers to alloys of low-melting point metals that manufacturers use to make fast, inexpensive castings.  The term "pot metal" came about due to the practice at automobile factories in the early 20th century of gathering up non-ferrous metal scraps from the manufacturing processes and melting them in one pot to form into cast products.  A small amount of iron usually made it into the castings, but too much iron raised the melting point, so it was minimized.

Queen Elizabeth Gate | Queen Elizabeth Gate, also known as the Queen Mother's Gate, is an entrance consisting of two pairs and two single gates of forged stainless steel and bronze situated in Hyde Park, London, behind Apsley House at Hyde Park Corner.  There is also a center feature made of painted cast iron.

361 Broadway | 361 Broadway at the corner of Franklin Street and Broadway in the Tribeca neighborhood of Manhattan, New York City, formerly known as the James White Building, was built in 1881-82 and was designed by W. Wheeler Smith in the Italianate style.  It features a cast-iron facade, and is a good example of late cast-iron architecture.  The building was renovated by architect Joseph Pell Lombardi in 2000, and a restoration of the facade began in 2009.
response.summary_2: The White Queen. Max Irons is an English-Irish actor who is known for his role in "The White Queen" (2013).
